summary: Gruber responds to Ed Bott's claim that Apple's iBooks Author represents an 'embrace, extend, extinguish' strategy against the ePub standard. He argues Apple is not being disingenuous because the iBooks Author format is clearly presented as proprietary — using a .ibooks extension, never calling itself ePub — making it fundamentally different from Microsoft's historical strategy. He draws a parallel to native iOS apps versus mobile web apps: Apple supports ePub as a cross-platform standard while offering a superior proprietary alternative. Gruber maintains his criticism of the restrictive EULA requiring iBookstore exclusivity, but defends Apple's right to create a proprietary format alongside ePub support.
key_points: Apple's iBooks Author format is clearly branded as proprietary (.ibooks extension, no ePub branding), making the embrace/extend/extinguish comparison inapt
The iBooks Author format relates to ePub the same way native iOS apps relate to mobile web apps — proprietary and superior alongside continued open standard support
There's a crucial distinction between supporting a cross-platform standard and committing to it as the only format
The EULA's iBookstore exclusivity requirement remains indefensible and represents Apple exerting maximum control
Gruber corrects his earlier assessment that the format was mostly HTML5, acknowledging it would take significant effort for competitors to render iBooks Author output
money_quotes: I agree with the greedy, but not the evil or the mind-bogglingliness.
The under-the-hood similarities to ePub are an implementation detail.

The only people who seem to be confused about iBooks Author's relationship to ePub are technically-minded people who know exactly what ePub is and who have a vested interest in seeing the open standard become the de facto industry standard.
There's a big difference between arguing 'There should be a good standard for cross-platform books' and 'All books should be based on a good cross-platform standard'.
I say this is Apple at its worst because Apple has chosen the path of exerting the most control.
A little more flexibility on Apple's part would do well for everyone, even Apple itself, as it would only further encourage the use of iBooks Author.

I think it would take significant effort for a competitor to render the output of iBooks Author with full or even near-full fidelity. All the more reason for Apple to remove or loosen the exclusivity clause of the EULA.
key_insight: Apple's iBooks Author format is legitimately proprietary rather than a subversion of ePub, but the restrictive EULA requiring iBookstore exclusivity is an unnecessary overreach that hurts adoption.
